questions.text: What is the primary enforcement mechanism of rules in a DAO?

options.text: Smart contracts that execute automatically based on code and voting outcomes.
A board of directors.
A government regulator.
Manual approval by the founder.

questions.explanation: DAOs use smart contracts to enforce rules. No single person can override a vote that has passed and been executed.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A

DAOs use smart contracts to enforce rules. No single person can override a vote that has passed and been executed.

questions.text: What is a governance token in a DAO?

options.text: A token that gives the holder voting rights on proposals.
A token used only for paying gas fees.
A stablecoin used in the treasury.
A token that automatically grants ownership of the DAO's assets.

questions.explanation: Governance tokens represent membership and voting power. They are typically ERC-20 tokens with delegation capabilities.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A

Governance tokens represent membership and voting power. They are typically ERC-20 tokens with delegation capabilities.

questions.text: What is the purpose of a timelock in a DAO?

options.text: To delay execution after a vote passes, giving members time to exit if they disagree.
To speed up proposal execution.
To count votes more accurately.
To prevent anyone from viewing the proposal.

questions.explanation: A timelock provides a safety buffer. After a proposal passes, it is queued in the timelock and can only be executed after a set period (e.g., 48 hours).
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A

A timelock provides a safety buffer. After a proposal passes, it is queued in the timelock and can only be executed after a set period (e.g., 48 hours).

questions.text: Which OpenZeppelin contract is the industry standard for building a DAO on EVM chains?

options.text: Governor (with TimelockController).
ERC20.
Ownable.
ReentrancyGuard.

questions.explanation: OpenZeppelin's Governor contract provides a full DAO framework including proposal lifecycle, voting, and execution. Combined with TimelockController, it's the standard for on-chain governance.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A

OpenZeppelin's Governor contract provides a full DAO framework including proposal lifecycle, voting, and execution. Combined with TimelockController, it's the standard for on-chain governance.

options.text: The minimum number of votes required for a proposal to be valid.
The maximum number of proposals per day.
The percentage of tokens that must be staked to create a proposal.
The number of times a proposal can be re-submitted.

questions.explanation: Quorum ensures that a minimum number of tokens participate in a vote, preventing a small group from passing a proposal with low turnout.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A

Quorum ensures that a minimum number of tokens participate in a vote, preventing a small group from passing a proposal with low turnout.

questions.text: Why are MST's low fees important for DAOs?

options.text: They make it affordable for all token holders to vote and participate, not just whales.
They allow DAOs to avoid using smart contracts.
They increase the value of governance tokens.
They make DAOs legally compliant.

questions.explanation: On expensive chains, voting costs can discourage participation. MST's sub-paisa fees democratize access to governance.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A

On expensive chains, voting costs can discourage participation. MST's sub-paisa fees democratize access to governance.

questions.text: What is "liquid democracy" in the context of DAOs?

options.text: Token holders can either vote directly or delegate their voting power to a trusted representative.
All decisions are made by a single AI.
Voting is done entirely off-chain.
Only token holders with a minimum balance can vote.

questions.explanation: Liquid democracy combines direct and representative democracy. You vote on issues you understand, and delegate on issues you don't.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A

Liquid democracy combines direct and representative democracy. You vote on issues you understand, and delegate on issues you don't.

questions.text: Which of the following is a common criticism of simple token-weighted voting (1 token = 1 vote)?

options.text: It can lead to plutocracy, where wealthy token holders dominate decisions.
It makes voting too fast.
It requires too much gas.
It allows anyone to create proposals for free.

questions.explanation: Simple token voting gives disproportionate power to large holders. Alternative mechanisms like quadratic voting aim to mitigate this.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A

Simple token voting gives disproportionate power to large holders. Alternative mechanisms like quadratic voting aim to mitigate this.

questions.text: What was the DAO hack of 2016?

options.text: An attacker exploited a reentrancy vulnerability in "The DAO" smart contract and drained $60 million worth of ETH.
A government seized the DAO's assets.
The founder stole all the tokens.
A bug caused all proposals to be deleted.

questions.explanation: The DAO hack was a pivotal moment that led to the Ethereum hard fork and highlighted the need for rigorous smart contract security.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A

The DAO hack was a pivotal moment that led to the Ethereum hard fork and highlighted the need for rigorous smart contract security.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a component of a typical DAO?

options.text: A centralized board of directors with veto power.
A governance token.
A treasury controlled by voting.
A proposal system.

questions.explanation: A DAO, by definition, has no central board. Governance is distributed among token holders.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A

A DAO, by definition, has no central board. Governance is distributed among token holders.
